data.राग: Reasearch to be done - Raag according to 22 shruti

data.How It Locks With Tanpura: The ONLY perfect lock; tanpura becomes completely calm and full
First low anchor; very small step from Sa
Second lock, warm stop of beats
Third lock, light brightness
Strongest, open ringing
Very low Ga; beats stop gently
Strong warm lock; “pulls heart inward”
Very clear, bell-like lock; tanpura becomes open
Slightly sharper than Shuddha Ga; fast beating near 5/4
Strong natural overtone (3rd harmonic)
Slightly sharp; beats almost stable but “tilted”
Very bright lock; tanpura overtone shines
Close to Pa; beats very fast
Clear overtone (perfect fifth); instantly locks
data.Raga Usage: All ragas except those without Pa (e.g., Malkauns)

data.How It Locks With Tanpura: Low, raw Dha; beats slow
Warm lock like Komal Ga; pulls downward
Neutral lock, not too sharp
Big, ringing, fast lock; tanpura expands
Low Ni; close beats, gentle lock
Warm lock like Komal Ga; inward pull
Strong overtone lock; tanpura “glows”
Nearly merges with Sa; tiny beats
Strong octave lock; complete silence of beats

data.Hindustani Music (North India): Melody (monophony), with an emphasis on expressive exploration of ragas.
data.Carnatic Music (South India): Melody (monophony), more composition-based within a strict raga framework.
data.Western Music: Harmony and counterpoint (polyphony), focusing on chords and vertical alignment of notes.

data.Hindustani Music (North India): Uses 12 semi-tones, but also incorporates subtle microtones (shruti) and fluid note transitions (meend).
data.Carnatic Music (South India): Recognizes 22 microtones (shruti) per octave, used with highly codified ornamentation (gamaka).
data.Western Music: Uses 12 fixed, equally tempered semitones per octave (e.g., in a piano).

data.Hindustani Music (North India): Emphasizes extended, free-flowing improvisation (alap, jod, jhala) that gradually builds in tempo and intensity.
data.Carnatic Music (South India): Built around pre-composed pieces called kritis, where improvisation occurs within specific sections of the composition.
data.Western Music: Primarily based on written compositions following a fixed form (e.g., sonatas, symphonies, concertos). Strict adherence to the score is expected.

data.Hindustani Music (North India): Features a variety of talas (rhythmic cycles) with scope for intricate improvisation.
data.Carnatic Music (South India): Known for highly complex and mathematically precise rhythmic patterns and calculations.
data.Western Music: Typically uses simpler, repetitive meters (e.g., 4/4, 3/4 time signatures).

data.Hindustani Music (North India): Evolved from ancient Vedic traditions with significant Persian and Mughal influences.
data.Carnatic Music (South India): Retained a more indigenous, ancient Hindu musical tradition with less external influence.
data.Western Music: Roots in European classical traditions, with secular origins and a focus on structure and precision.

data.Hindustani Music (North India): Sitar, sarod, tabla, harmonium, bansuri (flute), sarangi.
data.Western Music: Piano, violin, cello, trumpet, guitar, drums, orchestra.

data.Feature: Note: The primary differences between Carnatic, Hindustani, and Western music lie in their approach to melody, harmony, rhythm, improvisation, and cultural influences.

data.Main Purpose: To provide formal academic education combining theory and practice, grant officially recognized degrees, and prepare students for academic, research, and government cultural careers.
data.Description: These are full-fledged universities recognized by the University Grants Commission (UGC), a statutory body of the Indian government. Their degrees (B.A., M.A., B.P.A., M.P.A., Ph.D.) are officially sanctioned for employment in government and education sectors across India.

They offer comprehensive, formal academic programs that combine practical training with theoretical knowledge, research, history, aesthetics, and pedagogy. The curriculum is structured and often interdisciplinary.

A degree from a Tier 1 institution provides the highest level of official qualification for academic careers, such as university professors, researchers, and government cultural officers. It also opens doors to general employment opportunities requiring a formal university degree.
These are traditional university campuses with infrastructure, libraries, research facilities, and a diverse student body engaging in various academic and extracurricular activities.

data.Main Purpose: To offer performance-focused, practical training in music and arts through diplomas/certificates that enable teaching, professional performance, and local recognition within the arts community.
data.Description: These are private, non-profit organizations or cultural bodies. They do not grant university degrees directly but offer diplomas and certificates that are widely recognized and valued within the music and arts community. In many cases, their senior diplomas (like Sangeet Prabhakar) are considered equivalent to a B.A. or M.A. degree for the purpose of teaching in affiliated schools or certain government jobs.

The primary focus is often practical, performance-based training and a rigorous examination system with a vast network of affiliated local teaching centers. The learning experience is typically more practical and less theoretical than a university degree program.

Graduates often become music or dance teachers at private institutions, open their own local classes, or pursue professional performance careers. The "equivalency" of their diplomas to formal degrees can vary depending on the specific employer or state education board's rules.
They operate through a decentralized network of many smaller, affiliated centers, rather than a single, large university campus.

data.Main Purpose: To develop highly specialized skills in specific instruments, genres, or contemporary arts through internationally recognized graded examinations, mainly for professional performers and global opportunities.
data.Description: These include international bodies (like Trinity or ABRSM) or local specialized academies. Their primary focus is on standardized graded examinations that are globally recognized within the music industry and sometimes for higher education applications abroad (e.g., UCAS points for UK universities).

They provide a structured grading system focused purely on musical or artistic skill development and performance repertoire within specific genres (often Western music, pop, or contemporary styles). The focus is highly practical and skill-based.

These certifications are excellent for professional performers, private tutors, and individuals seeking international opportunities. They are generally not considered equivalent to a formal Indian university degree for domestic government employment or standard academic roles.
Highly specialized and niche, focusing on specific instruments,

data.Raga Name: Note: Perfect! Here’s a detailed reference chart for the Sarang raga family, including Aaroh, Avaroh, Vadi, Samvadi, Pakad, Time, and Mood. I’ve included the most commonly performed variants:
Aaroh/Avaroh: Defines the ascending and descending movement of notes.
Vadi/Samvadi: Dominant and secondary notes that give raga its identity.
Pakad: Signature phrase that helps listeners immediately recognize the raga.
Time/Mood: Traditional guidelines for best emotional effect.
Modifiers (Gaur, Brindabani, Shuddha, etc.): Distinguish specific variations within the Sarang family.
